name: Nausea / Vomiting / Gastroenteritis
summary: Acute self-limited nausea/vomiting, typically viral or foodborne, managed with antiemetics and oral rehydration in adults.

triage: Onset, frequency, and duration of vomiting; presence of diarrhea
Assess hydration: dizziness, urine output, dry mouth, lightheadedness on standing
Screen for blood/bile in emesis, severe or localized abdominal pain
Sick contacts, recent travel, suspect food source
Pregnancy status in females of childbearing age
nursing: Vitals including orthostatic BP/HR if dehydration suspected
Weight and general appearance/mucous membrane check
Quiet area with emesis basin; limit oral intake until antiemetic given
keyOrders: Oral rehydration trial (small frequent sips) after antiemetic
*IV fluids if available and moderate dehydration / failed PO trial
labs: *Urine pregnancy test if female of childbearing age (if indicated)
Electrolytes/CBC typically deferred to ER/outpatient if needed
imaging: *Defer abdominal imaging (CT/US) to ER if obstruction or surgical abdomen suspected
meds: Ondansetron 4 mg ODT/IV, may repeat once
*Promethazine 12.5-25 mg PO/PR if indicated (caution sedation)
*Metoclopramide 10 mg if indicated
Oral rehydration solution; advance to bland diet (BRAT) as tolerated
*IV normal saline bolus 500-1000 mL if dehydrated and IV available
reassess: Recheck ability to tolerate oral fluids 30-60 min after antiemetic
Reassess hydration status and vitals before discharge
Confirm no worsening or localizing abdominal pain
disposition: Discharge home if tolerating PO and stable vitals
PCP follow-up if symptoms persist beyond 48-72 h
Transfer to ER or call 911 if intractable vomiting, signs of severe dehydration/shock, bloody/bilious emesis, severe abdominal pain, or altered mental status
discharge: Oral rehydration instructions; small frequent clear fluids then bland diet
Continue antiemetic as prescribed; avoid dairy/fatty/spicy foods 24-48 h
Return precautions: persistent vomiting >24 h, no urine output, blood in emesis/stool, severe pain
followup: PCP if not improved in 48-72 h
Earlier follow-up for elderly, diabetic, or immunocompromised
*Stool studies via PCP if prolonged or bloody diarrhea

redFlags: Signs of severe dehydration or hemodynamic instability
Bilious or bloody (hematemesis/coffee-ground) vomiting
Severe, localized, or peritoneal abdominal pain
Altered mental status, severe headache, or stiff neck
Suspected bowel obstruction, GI bleed, DKA, or pregnancy complication
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; arrange EMTALA-appropriate ER transfer for unstable or surgical presentations
Document hydration assessment and PO tolerance prior to discharge
Provide clear written return precautions
Report suspected foodborne outbreaks to Texas DSHS as required
Limit antiemetics to short outpatient courses; counsel on driving/sedation

name: Diarrhea
summary: Acute diarrhea in adults, usually self-limited viral/bacterial, managed with rehydration and selective antimicrobials.

triage: Duration, frequency, and character of stools (watery vs bloody)
Associated fever, abdominal cramps, vomiting
Hydration assessment: thirst, urine output, dizziness
Recent travel, antibiotics, sick contacts, suspect foods
Immunocompromise, age, comorbidities
nursing: Vitals including orthostatic BP/HR if indicated
Collect stool sample if studies ordered
keyOrders: *IV fluids if available and dehydrated/failed PO
labs: *Stool occult blood / fecal studies if bloody or prolonged (if indicated)
*C. difficile testing deferred to outpatient if recent antibiotics
Electrolytes deferred to ER/outpatient if significant
imaging: *Defer abdominal CT to ER if severe pain or suspected colitis/obstruction
meds: Oral rehydration solution; advance diet as tolerated
Loperamide 4 mg then 2 mg per loose stool (max 16 mg/day) - avoid if bloody stool or high fever
*Bismuth subsalicylate if indicated
*Azithromycin 500 mg daily x3 d or ciprofloxacin for dysentery/traveler's diarrhea if indicated
*IV normal saline bolus if dehydrated and IV available
reassess: Reassess hydration and PO tolerance before discharge
Reconsider bacterial cause if high fever or bloody stools
disposition: Discharge home if stable and hydrated
PCP follow-up if persistent >7 days or recurrent
Transfer to ER or call 911 if severe dehydration/shock, significant GI bleeding, severe abdominal pain, high fever with toxic appearance, or altered mental status
discharge: Avoid antidiarrheals if bloody stool or fever
Diet advice: bland foods, avoid dairy/caffeine/alcohol short term
Return precautions: bloody stools, high fever, no urine output, severe pain, dizziness
followup: PCP if no improvement in 5-7 days or recurrent
*Stool studies/referral for chronic or bloody diarrhea

redFlags: Severe dehydration or hemodynamic instability
Grossly bloody diarrhea or signs of GI bleed
Severe or localized abdominal pain / peritoneal signs
High fever with toxic appearance or altered mental status
Immunocompromised host with significant illness
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; transfer unstable patients per EMTALA
Use antibiotics judiciously; avoid empiric antibiotics in suspected EHEC/bloody diarrhea
Report reportable enteric infections to Texas DSHS as required
Document hydration assessment and return precautions
Counsel on antidiarrheal contraindications

name: Constipation
summary: Adult functional constipation managed with bowel regimen and lifestyle counseling after excluding obstruction.

triage: Frequency and consistency of stools; duration of symptoms
Associated abdominal pain, distension, nausea/vomiting
Last bowel movement and flatus; any rectal bleeding
Medications (opioids, anticholinergics), fluid/fiber intake
Red-flag screen: weight loss, melena, new onset in older adult
nursing: Review medication list contributing to constipation
keyOrders: *Digital rectal exam for impaction if indicated
*Manual disimpaction / enema if fecal impaction and within scope
imaging: *Plain abdominal X-ray (KUB) if available to assess stool burden/obstruction
*Defer abdominal CT to ER if obstruction suspected
meds: Polyethylene glycol 17 g in 8 oz fluid daily
*Docusate 100 mg BID and/or senna 8.6-17.2 mg at bedtime if indicated
*Bisacodyl 10 mg PO/PR if indicated
*Glycerin suppository or saline enema for distal stool if indicated
Increase fiber, fluids, and activity; supplement with psyllium
reassess: Reassess for relief if enema/disimpaction performed
Confirm no obstructive features (vomiting, severe distension)
disposition: Discharge home with bowel regimen if stable and no obstruction
PCP follow-up for chronic/recurrent constipation or red flags
Transfer to ER or call 911 if suspected bowel obstruction, severe pain, significant rectal bleeding, vomiting, or peritoneal signs
discharge: Bowel regimen instructions and expected timeline
Increase dietary fiber, fluids, and physical activity
Return precautions: severe pain, vomiting, distension, no BM/flatus, rectal bleeding
followup: PCP for persistent or chronic constipation
GI referral for alarm features (weight loss, bleeding, anemia, age >50 new onset)

redFlags: Suspected bowel obstruction (vomiting, distension, no flatus)
Severe or peritoneal abdominal pain
Significant rectal bleeding or melena
Unintentional weight loss or new-onset constipation in older adult
Systemic signs of toxicity
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; transfer suspected obstruction per EMTALA
Document exclusion of obstruction before laxative therapy
Refer age-appropriate patients for colorectal cancer screening and alarm-feature workup
Provide written bowel-regimen and return precautions
Avoid chronic stimulant laxative overuse counseling

name: Mild Abdominal Pain
summary: Evaluation of mild, non-acute abdominal pain in adults to identify benign causes and exclude surgical emergencies.

triage: Pain location, onset, character, radiation, and severity (mild = able to function)
Associated nausea/vomiting, fever, urinary or bowel changes
Relation to meals; last BM; trauma history
Pregnancy status in females of childbearing age
Screen red flags: severe/worsening pain, rigidity, syncope
nursing: Focused abdominal exam observation; note guarding/rebound for provider
labs: *Urine pregnancy test if female of childbearing age
CBC/CMP/lipase typically deferred to ER if surgical cause suspected
imaging: *Plain abdominal X-ray if available for constipation/free air screen (limited)
Defer abdominal/pelvic CT or ultrasound to ER or outpatient
*Outpatient US referral for gallbladder/pelvic causes if stable
meds: Acetaminophen 650-1000 mg for analgesia
*Antacid/PPI (e.g., omeprazole 20 mg daily) if dyspepsia suspected
*Antispasmodic (dicyclomine 20 mg) for cramping if indicated
*Treat identified cause (e.g., antibiotics for confirmed UTI)
Avoid opioids; avoid NSAIDs if gastritis/ulcer suspected
reassess: Reassess pain and exam after analgesia
Confirm no progression, rebound, guarding, or worsening vitals
Verify oral tolerance and ability to ambulate
disposition: Discharge home if mild, stable, benign exam, and reassuring vitals
PCP or specialist (GI/surgery) follow-up for persistent symptoms
Transfer to ER or call 911 if severe/worsening pain, peritoneal signs, hemodynamic instability, suspected appendicitis/obstruction/ectopic, or GI bleeding
discharge: Specific return precautions for worsening or localized pain
Return precautions: severe/persistent pain, fever, vomiting, bleeding, fainting
followup: PCP within a few days if symptoms persist
GI/surgical referral for recurrent or unexplained pain

redFlags: Severe, sudden, or progressively worsening pain
Peritoneal signs: rigidity, rebound, involuntary guarding
Hemodynamic instability, syncope, or pallor
Suspected appendicitis, obstruction, perforation, AAA, or ectopic pregnancy
GI bleeding (hematemesis, melena, hematochezia) or intractable vomiting
compliance: Urgent care is not a freestanding ER; transfer surgical/unstable abdomens per EMTALA
Always document pregnancy testing in reproductive-age females
Avoid masking serious pathology; do not over-medicate before exam
Provide explicit written return precautions and timely follow-up
Exercise controlled-substance prudence; avoid opioids for undifferentiated abdominal pain
